title: Celtic Battle Trance and Modern Body Wisdom
dek: *The berserker fury of Celtic tradition wasn't madness but a deliberate somatic technology. When you reclaim the body's capacity for righteous fire, you recover an ancient birthright: the ability to know what you know, defend what you love, and move through the world with the full voltage of your nervous system intact.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Ancient Celtic warriors used controlled rage states to access superhuman strength, and modern somatic research reveals the same physiology still lives in your nervous system. Suppressed anger creates muscular tension, elevated blood pressure, and dissociation from the body's protective intelligence. Learning to channel - not suppress - this energy restores vitality, boundary clarity, and the felt sense of your own aliveness.

She sat through the meeting nodding, smiling even, while her colleague took credit for the third iteration of her idea. Her jaw stayed loose. Her breathing stayed shallow and high in her chest. She noticed her left foot had gone numb under the table, and later, driving home, she realized she couldn't remember a single word spoken in the last twenty minutes. The anger was there - somewhere below the clavicle, maybe deeper - but it had no name, no shape, no permission to rise. ## The Fian and the Physiology of Battle Heat The Fianna were elite warrior bands in ancient Ireland, and their training included something the medieval texts called *ferg* or *ríastrad* - a deliberate entering into battle trance. This wasn't random violence. It was a cultivated state, accessed through breath work, chanting, physical posturing, and what we'd now recognize as somatic activation. The warriors would work themselves into a controlled fury that allowed them to override fear, access reserve strength, and fight with a clarity that came not from the thinking mind but from the body's own fierce intelligence. In our experience, most people today have been taught the exact opposite skill: how to suppress the first flicker of heat before it registers as anything nameable. We've been rewarded for staying pleasant, for keeping the voice even, for not making waves. But the nervous system doesn't forget. Peter Levine, whose work on trauma and the body has shaped modern somatic therapy, points out that incomplete defensive responses - moments when we wanted to push back, run, or roar but didn't - get stored as muscular tension and dysregulation. The fight impulse that never completes becomes the tight shoulders, the clenched jaw, the chronic neck pain that no amount of massage seems to touch. The Celts understood something we're only now re-learning through polyvagal theory and somatic psychology: the body holds its own form of knowing, and sometimes that knowing is hot, sharp, and entirely appropriate. ## What Happens When Fire Has No Exit Karla McLaren, who writes about the intelligence of emotions, describes anger as a boundary-setting force. It tells you when something is wrong, when a limit has been crossed, when action is required. When that signal gets jammed - when you're taught that anger is "ugly" or "unspiritual" or "too much" - the message doesn't disappear. It just goes underground. Over the years I've seen this pattern again and again: the person who never raises their voice ends up with migraines. The one who prides themselves on being endlessly accommodating develops gut issues that no specialist can explain. The body, denied one language, will find another. Research published on PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows that suppressed anger correlates with elevated blood pressure, increased cortisol, and a host of inflammatory markers. The system stays revved with nowhere to go. Thomas Moore, in his reflections on the soul's darker moods, suggests that we've lost the ritual containers that used to hold these energies. Ancient cultures had war dances, drumming circles, initiatory ordeals. We have traffic jams and passive-aggressive email threads. The fire is the same. The outlets have withered. When you disconnect from the body's anger, you also disconnect from its vitality. The same neural pathways that carry rage also carry passion, creative force, the capacity to say a clear *no* and mean it. Shut down one, and you mute the whole channel. ## Somatic Markers: Reading the Body's Battle Signs Your body announces anger long before your mind catches up. There's a quality of heat that rises in the chest or throat. The hands may clench or tingle. The jaw sets. The breath changes - shorter, higher, faster. In the moment, these sensations can feel uncomfortable, even frightening, especially if you've spent years learning to override them. But these are not malfunctions. They're information. The Celtic warriors trained to notice these signs and ride them rather than resist them. Modern somatic work teaches the same skill. You don't have to act on every flicker of heat, but you do need to *feel* it. The noticing itself begins to complete the circuit that suppression leaves open. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return to this principle: the body is not the problem. The body is the solution. When you can track sensation without immediately making it mean something ("I'm a bad person for feeling this," "I should be over this by now"), you start to recover a kind of animal intelligence that our culture has worked very hard to erase. Here's what that looks like in practice. You're in a conversation and suddenly there's heat in your throat, a tightness across the shoulders. Instead of pushing it down or immediately speaking, you pause. You breathe into the sensation. You ask it, silently, *What do you need me to know?* Sometimes the answer is immediate: *This person just lied to you.* Sometimes it's slower: *You've been letting this slide for months.* Either way, you're back in your body, back in contact with your own knowing. ## A Five-Minute Practice: The Warrior's Breath You can do this standing or seated, but standing tends to wake the body's older circuits more directly. Plant your feet hip-width apart. Let your knees soften slightly, not locked. Take a full breath in through the nose, filling the belly first, then the ribs, then the chest. Hold it for a beat. Then exhale sharply through the mouth with sound - *hah* - engaging the belly, the diaphragm, the whole core. Do this three times, letting the exhale get louder and more forceful each round. Now shake out your hands like you're flinging water off them. Shake your arms, your shoulders, your whole torso if that feels right. Let your jaw hang loose and make whatever sound wants to come - groan, sigh, growl, whatever. This isn't about being polite. This is about letting the nervous system discharge some of the static it's been holding. After a minute or so, stand still. Notice what's different. You might feel more awake, more present, maybe a little warmer. That's the beginning of coming back online. ## The Herbal Allies of Balanced Fire The body's capacity to feel and move anger depends partly on the nervous system's baseline state. If you're chronically depleted, running on fumes, even small provocations can tip you into shutdown or explosion because there's no buffer, no resilience. Some of the plants we keep in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) work specifically with this edge. Milky oats, for instance, feed the nervous system over time, rebuilding the capacity to handle intensity without collapsing. Skullcap helps when the mind won't stop looping on the same grievance, offering a kind of gentle release valve. Passionflower can support the transition from wired-but-numb to genuinely felt and present. For moments when the internal pressure feels unmanageable, a magnesium supplement like [Pure Encapsulations Magnesium Glycinate, 90 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07P5K7DQP?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help ease the muscular tension that holds the anger in place. If you're working with patterns of irritability that never quite land, something like [Source Naturals Serene Science 5-HTP, Balanced Mood 100 mg - 30 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0010ZWJMQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) may help stabilize the serotonin baseline that lets you access the full emotional range without getting stuck at either extreme. And when you need support transitioning out of a heightened state, [Pure Encapsulations Pure Tranquility Liquid](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008EKVG3I?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a gentle landing. None of these replace the somatic work. But they can create the conditions in which that work becomes possible. ## Reclaiming the Trance State in Daily Life You don't need to charge into literal battle to access the focused intensity the Celts cultivated. You need moments when you're fully inhabiting your body, fully present to what you feel, willing to let the energy move. This might look like a hard workout where you let yourself grunt, growl, push past the point of polite exertion. It might be dancing alone in your living room to music that makes you want to stomp. It might be hitting a heavy bag, or chopping wood, or any activity that lets the body discharge heat through movement rather than words. The point isn't catharsis for its own sake. It's about re-establishing the feedback loop between sensation and action, between inner state and outer expression. When that loop is intact, you don't accumulate the slow poison of unfelt rage. You feel it, you move it, and it clears. In my own practice, I've found that even five minutes of deliberate physical intensity - pushing against a wall as hard as I can, doing burpees until my lungs burn, shaking my whole body like a wet dog - can shift a mood that talk therapy would take weeks to touch. The body knows how to complete what the mind keeps rehearsing. ## The Difference Between Rage and Clarity One of the fears people bring to this work is that if they let themselves feel anger fully, they'll lose control, hurt someone, become the thing they've spent their lives trying not to be. But the Celtic battle trance wasn't about losing control. It was about entering a different *kind* of control, one rooted in the body's own intelligence rather than the socializing mind's endless second-guessing. True rage - the kind that harms - often comes from the same source as complete suppression: a severance from the body's feedback. When you can't feel the early signals, the pressure builds until it explodes. When you *can* feel them, you have options. You can speak. You can leave. You can set a boundary before resentment calcifies into bitterness. The tools we share in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) are designed to help you find that middle path - not numb, not exploding, but awake and responsive. It's the difference between a wildfire and a controlled burn. Both are hot. Only one is useful. ## Modern Initiations and the Return of the Warrior We don't have formalized rites of passage anymore, no cultural containers that teach young people how to meet their own power without apology. But the need hasn't gone anywhere. If anything, it's more urgent. A culture that pathologizes anger while simultaneously marinating in passive aggression and ambient rage is a culture that's lost the thread. You can see this playing out everywhere - in comment sections, in burnout rates, in the quiet epidemic of people who feel nothing and don't know why. The energy has to go somewhere. If it's not given conscious form, it finds unconscious outlets: addiction, chronic illness, a life that feels dulled at the edges no matter what you achieve. The return of the warrior isn't about violence. It's about the willingness to *feel* what you feel and act from that place with integrity. It's about knowing the difference between a boundary and a wall, between force and violence, between the heat that protects and the heat that destroys. Some of the lineages explored on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) touch this same territory from different angles - the sacred masculine, the reclamation of instinct, the path that doesn't bypass the body on the way to the spirit. All of it points back to the same insight: you are not too much. You were never too much. You were taught to be less. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it safe to work with anger if I have a history of explosive outbursts? Yes, but the approach matters. Somatic work isn't about strengthening explosiveness - it's about building the capacity to feel intensity *without* bypassing into action. If you've had patterns of lashing out, it often means the anger bypassed the felt-sense stage and went straight to discharge. Working with a somatic therapist or using grounding practices can help you slow down that circuit, so you experience the sensation, track it, and choose your response rather than reacting from a hijacked state. ### How do I know if I'm suppressing anger or if I'm just not an angry person? One way to check: notice if you have chronic tension in your jaw, neck, or shoulders. Notice if you people-please, over-apologize, or have trouble saying no. Notice if you feel numb, flat, or disconnected from your body most of the time. These are often signs that anger (and other intense emotions) are being suppressed before they reach conscious awareness. Truly integrated people can access the full range of feeling - including anger - when the situation calls for it, and then let it go when it's served its purpose. ### Can I do this work without a therapist or guide? You can begin on your own, especially with practices like the warrior's breath, movement, and body awareness. But if you have significant trauma history, complex PTSD, or a pattern of dissociation, working with someone trained in somatic modalities (Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, EMDR) will give you a safer container. The body holds a lot, and sometimes what comes up needs a skilled witness. Start gently, track your window of tolerance, and reach out for support if things feel overwhelming. ## The Long Memory of the Body There's a passage in one of the old Irish texts where a warrior is described as having "the heat of seven men" in battle. Modern readers tend to take this as poetic exaggeration, but the Celts were often more literal than we give them credit for. They understood that the body, fully inhabited and unafraid of its own intensity, can access reserves of strength and clarity that the domesticated self never touches. You carry that same capacity. It's not gone. It's not broken. It's been waiting, sometimes for decades, for permission to come back online. The work isn't about becoming someone new. It's about remembering what your body has always known: that you are allowed to take up space, to defend what matters, to burn when burning is called for and rest when rest is needed. And here's the gift that comes with that remembering - when you're no longer spending energy suppressing your own aliveness, that energy becomes available for everything else. For creativity, for joy, for the kind of presence that doesn't have to be performed because it's just what's true. The warrior's trance isn't a destination. It's a doorway back into the fullness of being human, with all the heat and complexity and fierce tenderness that entails. When you walk through it, you don't leave anything behind. You bring it all with you, finally whole. ## Keep reading - [the assessments hub](/assessments) - [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) - [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on celtic battle trance and modern body wisdom, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
